entries.title: The audit log was cleared
entries.summary: Security log cleared.
entries.details: High severity because it may indicate evidence destruction. Correlate with System 104 for System log clearing.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName identifies the account that cleared the log — flag any non-IT account
Correlate with System EID 104 — simultaneous clearing of both Security and System logs is a strong evidence destruction indicator
Review the timestamp and check for any log entries from just before the clear for an attacker timeline
commonFalsePositives: IT administrators clearing logs for storage management (rare, should be in change records)

detectionRules.title: Security Log Cleared
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 1102
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Every occurrence is high priority — there is no legitimate automated reason to clear the Security log outside of approved change windows.

detectionRules.title: Security Log Cleared
detectionRules.rule:
title: Security Log Cleared
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 1102
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrator log management
level: high

entries.title: The system time was changed
entries.summary: System clock was modified.
entries.details: Generated when the system clock is changed. Key fields: SubjectUserName (who changed the time), PreviousTime, and NewTime. Attackers may modify the system clock to manipulate log timestamps, evade time-based detection rules, or shift the investigative timeline. A time change that moves the clock backward — especially by hours — immediately before or after malicious activity is a strong defense evasion indicator. Correlate with Security EID 4688 to identify the process that invoked the time change.

investigationPivots: Compare PreviousTime and NewTime — large backward shifts immediately before malicious activity suggest deliberate timestamp manipulation
SubjectUserName identifies who changed the time — non-admin accounts or unexpected accounts warrant investigation
Correlate with Security EID 4688 around the same timestamp to identify what process triggered the time change
commonFalsePositives: NTP synchronization events, especially on systems with clock drift after extended downtime
VM host/guest time synchronization on virtual machines

entries.title: An account was successfully logged on
entries.summary: Successful logon session creation.
entries.details: Used to validate interactive, network, service, and batch logons. Pivot on LogonType, TargetUserName, and source address.

investigationPivots: LogonType is the primary pivot: 2=interactive (console), 3=network (SMB/WMI), 10=RemoteInteractive (RDP), 4=batch, 5=service
IpAddress/WorkstationName identifies the source — flag logons from unexpected hosts or external IPs
TargetUserName combined with LogonType 3 from an admin account outside business hours is a common lateral movement pattern
Correlate LogonID with EID 4634/4647 to track session duration
commonFalsePositives: Normal user and service account logons
High-frequency network logons (LogonType 3) from backup, monitoring, or endpoint management systems

detectionRules.title: Suspicious Network Logon Outside Business Hours
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4624
| where LogonType == 3
| where AccountType != "Machine"
| where TargetUserName !endswith "$"
| where hourofday(TimeGenerated) !between (7 .. 18)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, TargetUserName, TargetDomainName, IpAddress, WorkstationName, LogonType
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Tune business hours window and exclude known service accounts and backup systems to reduce volume.

detectionRules.title: RDP Logon (LogonType 10) from External IP
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4624
| where LogonType == 10
| where IpAddress !startswith "10." and IpAddress !startswith "192.168." and IpAddress !startswith "172."
| where IpAddress != "-" and IpAddress != "127.0.0.1"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, TargetUserName, TargetDomainName, IpAddress, LogonType
| order by TimeGenerated desc

detectionRules.title: Logon with Explicit Credentials from Unusual Source
detectionRules.rule:
title: Suspicious Interactive Logon
status: experimental
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4624
    LogonType:
      - 2
      - 10
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Normal user desktop logons
level: informational

entries.title: An account failed to log on
entries.summary: Failed logon attempt recorded.
entries.details: Useful for brute force or password spray detection. Pivot on Status/SubStatus and source address.

investigationPivots: SubStatus 0xC000006A (wrong password) vs 0xC0000064 (bad username) distinguishes valid-user brute force from username enumeration
Many failures against many accounts from one source = password spray; many failures against one account = brute force
IpAddress/WorkstationName identifies the source — external IPs or unfamiliar workstations are high priority
Correlate with EID 4740 to identify accounts that were locked out as a result
commonFalsePositives: Misconfigured service accounts using stale credentials
Users failing to log on after a password change before updating saved credentials

detectionRules.title: Password Spray Detection — Many Accounts from One Source
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4625
| where LogonType in (3, 10)
| summarize FailureCount = count(), DistinctAccounts = dcount(TargetUserName) by IpAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 10m)
| where DistinctAccounts >= 5 and FailureCount >= 10
| project TimeGenerated, IpAddress, FailureCount, DistinctAccounts
| order by FailureCount desc
detectionRules.notes: Thresholds should be tuned per environment. Set DistinctAccounts lower (e.g., 3) in smaller environments.

detectionRules.title: Brute Force Detection — Many Failures Against One Account
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4625
| summarize FailureCount = count() by TargetUserName, IpAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where FailureCount >= 20
| project TimeGenerated, TargetUserName, IpAddress, FailureCount
| order by FailureCount desc

detectionRules.title: Multiple Failed Logon Attempts
detectionRules.rule:
title: Multiple Failed Logon Attempts (Brute Force)
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4625
  condition: selection | count() by IpAddress > 20
  timeframe: 5m
falsepositives:
  - Misconfigured service accounts with stale credentials
level: medium

entries.title: An account was logged off
entries.summary: Logon session terminated.
entries.details: Generated when a logon session ends and no longer exists. Correlate with 4624 using LogonID to track session duration. Note: 4647 is generated for user-initiated interactive/RDP logoffs; 4634 marks session termination.

investigationPivots: LogonID links this event back to EID 4624 — use it to calculate full session duration
Long-lived sessions (hours to days) for network logon types may indicate persistent attacker access or a long-running tool
commonFalsePositives: Normal session termination for any logon type

entries.title: User initiated logoff
entries.summary: User-initiated interactive logoff recorded.
entries.details: Generated when a user initiates a logoff from an interactive or RDP session. Unlike 4634 which marks session termination, 4647 records the user's intent to log off. Correlate with 4634 using LogonID to confirm session end.

investigationPivots: Correlate LogonID with EID 4624 to identify the originating logon and calculate session duration
EID 4647 without a subsequent EID 4634 may indicate the session terminated abnormally before the logon record was closed
commonFalsePositives: Normal user-initiated logoff from interactive or RDP sessions

entries.title: A logon was attempted using explicit credentials
entries.summary: Explicit credential logon attempt recorded.
entries.details: Generated when a process uses explicit credentials such as runas or scheduled task authentication. Pivot on SubjectUserName, TargetUserName, TargetServerName, and ProcessName. Common in lateral movement and privilege escalation scenarios.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName vs TargetUserName mismatch indicates credential substitution — flag when TargetUserName is a more privileged account
TargetServerName identifies the remote target — flag connections to domain controllers, servers, or internal infrastructure
ProcessName of unexpected executables (e.g., cmd.exe, wmic.exe, PowerShell) using explicit credentials is a lateral movement indicator
commonFalsePositives: Administrators using runas to run tools under a different account
Scheduled tasks configured with explicit credentials triggering at execution time

detectionRules.title: Explicit Credential Use by Non-Admin Process
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4648
| where SubjectUserName != TargetUserName
| where ProcessName !in ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\lsass.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\services.exe")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, TargetUserName, TargetServerName, ProcessName
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Filter known scheduled task and service manager processes. Focus on ProcessName values outside System32 or unusual locations.

detectionRules.title: Explicit Credential Logon
detectionRules.rule:
title: Explicit Credential Logon Attempt
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4648
  filter:
    SubjectUserName|endswith: '$'
  condition: selection and not filter
falsepositives:
  - Administrators using runas
  - Scheduled tasks using explicit credentials
level: medium

entries.title: A Kerberos service ticket was requested with a replayed authentication
entries.summary: Kerberos replay attack detected.
entries.details: Generated when the Kerberos Key Distribution Center detects a replayed authenticator — an authenticator previously used in a valid ticket request being resubmitted. This event fires when an attacker captures a valid Kerberos authenticator via network interception and re-uses it before it expires. Because Kerberos authenticators have a timestamp-based window (default 5 minutes), this event is only generated when replay detection succeeds. Key fields: TargetUserName and ClientAddress identify the requesting account and source. The presence of this event indicates either an active replay attack or a significant time skew issue that is triggering false replay detection.

prerequisites.description: Audit Account Logon > Audit Kerberos Service Ticket Operations must be enabled (Success and Failure)

investigationPivots: ClientAddress identifies the source of the replay — pivot to this host for further investigation
TargetUserName reveals which account's ticket was replayed — correlate with recent 4768/4769 requests from the same account
A cluster of this event from different sources targeting the same account indicates a credential relay or ticket sharing attack
commonFalsePositives: Severe clock skew (more than 5 minutes) between client and KDC causing authenticators to appear replayed
Network packet retransmissions in lossy environments can sometimes trigger replay detection

entries.title: A handle to an object was requested
entries.summary: Object handle request recorded.
entries.details: Generated when access to a file, registry key, or kernel object is requested. Records the requested access rights but not whether the operation was performed — correlate with 4663 to confirm execution. Requires SACL configuration on monitored objects.

investigationPivots: AccessMask field records what access was requested — flag WriteData, DELETE, or WRITE_DAC on sensitive objects
ObjectName identifies the file or registry key — correlate against a list of sensitive paths (SAM, NTDS, LSASS, credential stores)
Correlate with EID 4663 to confirm whether the handle was used for actual access
commonFalsePositives: Normal file and registry access by legitimate applications — high volume on systems without targeted SACLs

entries.title: A registry value was modified
entries.summary: Registry value was created or changed.
entries.details: Generated when a registry value is created, modified, or deleted, provided object access auditing is enabled for the specific registry key. Key fields: SubjectUserName, ObjectName (the registry key path), ValueName, OldValue, and NewValue. High-value registry paths for DFIR include Run/RunOnce keys (persistence), service ImagePath values (service hijacking), Defender and security tool configuration keys (tamper), and application compatibility shims (persistence). This event is noisy and should be filtered to known-sensitive registry paths.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

investigationPivots: ObjectName is the key pivot — filter on Run/RunOnce, service ImagePath, Defender exclusion paths, and security tool configuration keys
OldValue vs NewValue reveals what changed — value deletions (OldValue present, NewValue absent) can also indicate cleanup
Correlate with Security EID 4688 to identify the process that made the change
commonFalsePositives: High-volume legitimate registry activity — this event requires targeted filtering on sensitive key paths to be actionable
Software installations and updates modifying their own registry keys

entries.title: An operation was performed on an object
entries.summary: An operation was performed on an Active Directory object.
entries.details: Generated when an operation is performed on an Active Directory object, such as reading, writing, or replicating a directory object. Critical for detecting DCSync attacks: when an attacker uses replication rights to request password hashes from a DC, the DC generates EID 4662 with ObjectType of DS-Replication-Get-Changes and DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All. Key fields: SubjectUserName, ObjectDN (the object's distinguished name), ObjectType, and OperationType. Filter on replication-related GUIDs to detect DCSync from non-DC accounts.

investigationPivots: DCSync detection: filter for ObjectType GUIDs 1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2 (DS-Replication-Get-Changes) and 1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2 (DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All) from a non-DC account
SubjectUserName should be a domain controller machine account for legitimate replication — any other account is suspicious
Correlate with Security EID 4624 to identify how the attacker authenticated before performing the replication request
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate AD replication between domain controllers — SubjectUserName will be a DC machine account
Azure AD Connect and other directory synchronization tools with replication permissions

entries.title: An attempt was made to access an object
entries.summary: Object access attempt recorded.
entries.details: Generated when a specific access right is exercised on a file, registry key, or kernel object with a matching SACL. Pivot on ObjectName, SubjectUserName, and Accesses. Requires SACL configuration on monitored objects to generate.

investigationPivots: Accesses field shows what operation was performed — WriteData/AddFile or DELETE on sensitive paths are high priority
ObjectName identifies the specific file or registry key accessed — flag sensitive paths such as SAM, NTDS.dit, credential manager stores
SubjectUserName combined with ProcessName reveals what account and process performed the access
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate application access to monitored files — high volume without targeted SACL scoping

entries.title: Permissions on an object were changed
entries.summary: Object DACL/permissions changed.
entries.details: Generated when the permissions (DACL) of a file, registry key, or token object are changed. OldSd and NewSd fields contain SDDL representations of the change. Requires SACL configuration on monitored objects.

investigationPivots: OldSd and NewSd are SDDL strings — diff them to determine what permissions were added or removed
Permission changes on security tool directories, log directories, or credential stores may indicate defense evasion or pre-staging
SubjectUserName combined with ObjectName identifies who changed permissions on what
commonFalsePositives: Software installers modifying file or registry permissions during setup
Group Policy applying permission changes to managed objects

entries.title: Special privileges assigned to new logon
entries.summary: Sensitive privileges assigned at logon.
entries.details: Generated when a new logon session is assigned sensitive privileges such as SeDebugPrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege, or SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege. Very common for SYSTEM logons. Monitor for unexpected accounts with high-value privileges in PrivilegeList.

investigationPivots: PrivilegeList is the key field — SeDebugPrivilege enables memory access to other processes (used by credential dumpers), SeBackupPrivilege enables reading any file
Correlate LogonID with EID 4624 to identify the logon session that received the privileges
Unexpected non-admin accounts assigned SeDebugPrivilege or SeTcbPrivilege are high-confidence escalation indicators
commonFalsePositives: SYSTEM logons are very high volume and routinely receive sensitive privileges — baseline and filter these
Administrator logons receiving their normal assigned privileges

detectionRules.title: Sensitive Privilege Assigned to Non-Admin Account
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4672
| where AccountName !endswith "$"
| where AccountName !in ("SYSTEM", "LOCAL SERVICE", "NETWORK SERVICE")
| where PrivilegeList has_any ("SeDebugPrivilege", "SeLoadDriverPrivilege", "SeTcbPrivilege", "SeCreateTokenPrivilege", "SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, AccountName, AccountDomain, PrivilegeList
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: SYSTEM logons are extremely high-volume and routinely receive these privileges — exclude AccountName == 'SYSTEM' or baseline those first.

entries.title: A privileged service was called
entries.summary: Sensitive privilege used by a process.
entries.details: Generated when a process uses a sensitive Windows privilege such as SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege, SeCreateTokenPrivilege, SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege, or SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege. Key fields: SubjectUserName, ProcessName, and PrivilegeList. SeDebugPrivilege is the most significant DFIR indicator — it is required for direct LSASS memory access (Mimikatz sekurlsa::logonpasswords, Invoke-Mimikatz, ProcDump -ma lsass.exe). SeLoadDriverPrivilege is used to load unauthorized kernel drivers. SeTcbPrivilege grants the process 'act as part of the operating system' rights. Unexpected processes holding or using these privileges outside of security software, system processes, and known admin tools warrant immediate investigation.

prerequisites.description: Audit Privilege Use > Audit Sensitive Privilege Use must be enabled (Success and Failure)

investigationPivots: PrivilegeList is the key field — filter for SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege, and SeCreateTokenPrivilege
ProcessName identifies the binary claiming the privilege — any process outside System32 or known security software using SeDebugPrivilege is high priority
Correlate SubjectUserName with EID 4688 to build the process ancestry chain — unexpected parent processes are a strong indicator
commonFalsePositives: Security software (AV, EDR, vulnerability scanners) legitimately using SeDebugPrivilege for memory inspection
Task Manager and Process Explorer using SeDebugPrivilege for process inspection by administrators

detectionRules.title: SeDebugPrivilege Used by Non-System Process
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4673
| where PrivilegeList has "SeDebugPrivilege"
| where ProcessName !has "\\Windows\\"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, ProcessName, PrivilegeList
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Requires Audit Sensitive Privilege Use enabled. SeDebugPrivilege is the primary Mimikatz/credential dumper indicator. Tune by allowlisting known security tools.

detectionRules.title: Sensitive Privilege Use — SeDebugPrivilege
detectionRules.rule:
title: Sensitive Privilege Use — SeDebugPrivilege
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4673
    PrivilegeList|contains: 'SeDebugPrivilege'
  filter:
    ProcessName|startswith:
      - 'C:\Windows\'
      - 'C:\Program Files\'
  condition: selection and not filter
falsepositives:
  - Security software performing memory inspection
  - Task Manager or Process Explorer run by administrators
level: high

entries.title: An operation was attempted on a privileged object
entries.summary: An operation was attempted using a sensitive privilege.
entries.details: Generated when an operation is attempted on a privileged object such as a security-sensitive API call or token operation that uses a sensitive privilege. Closely related to EID 4673, which records privilege use — EID 4674 is more specific to the object-level operation. Key fields: SubjectUserName, ObjectServer, ObjectType, ObjectName, and PrivilegeList. This event fires for operations on objects requiring elevated privileges like LSASS handles, token duplication, or security descriptor changes on privileged objects. Combined with EID 4673, a cluster of these events from an unexpected process is strong evidence of credential dumping or privilege escalation.

prerequisites.description: Audit Privilege Use > Audit Sensitive Privilege Use must be enabled (Success and Failure)

investigationPivots: Correlate ObjectName and PrivilegeList — LSASS as object with SeDebugPrivilege is the highest fidelity credential dumping indicator
Combine with EID 4673 from the same SubjectUserName and ProcessName for a complete privilege use chain
Correlate with Sysmon EID 10 (ProcessAccess) for a cross-source view of the same operation
commonFalsePositives: Security software performing legitimate process inspection or memory scanning
System processes and services performing normal privileged operations at boot and during updates

entries.title: A new process has been created
entries.summary: Process creation event.
entries.details: High-value telemetry when command-line logging is enabled. Pivot on ParentProcessName and CommandLine.

investigationPivots: CommandLine (requires audit policy: Include command line in process creation events) is the highest-value field — search for encoded commands, download cradles, and LOLBAS abuse (binaries such as certutil.exe and mshta.exe, scripts via wscript.exe/cscript.exe, and libraries loaded through rundll32.exe)
ParentProcessName establishes the process chain — unexpected parents (e.g., Office spawning cmd.exe, or wscript.exe spawning PowerShell) are strong malicious indicators
SubjectUserName identifies the account context — SYSTEM or service account spawning interactive shells is suspicious
Correlate NewProcessId with EID 4689 to track process lifetime
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate process creation — very high volume; effective use requires focused filtering on suspicious parent-child relationships or command patterns

detectionRules.title: Suspicious Parent-Child Process Relationship
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4688
| where ParentProcessName has_any ("winword.exe", "excel.exe", "powerpnt.exe", "outlook.exe", "mshta.exe", "wscript.exe", "cscript.exe")
| where NewProcessName has_any ("cmd.exe", "powershell.exe", "wmic.exe", "rundll32.exe", "regsvr32.exe", "certutil.exe")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, NewProcessName, ParentProcessName, CommandLine
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Requires 'Include command line in process creation events' audit policy for CommandLine field. Adjust parent/child lists to environment.

detectionRules.title: Encoded PowerShell Command Execution
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4688
| where NewProcessName endswith "\\powershell.exe" or NewProcessName endswith "\\pwsh.exe"
| where CommandLine has_any ("-enc", "-EncodedCommand", "-e ", "FromBase64String")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, CommandLine, ParentProcessName
| order by TimeGenerated desc

detectionRules.title: LOLBAS Execution via Process Creation
detectionRules.rule:
title: LOLBAS Execution via Process Creation
status: experimental
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4688
    NewProcessName|endswith:
      - '\certutil.exe'
      - '\mshta.exe'
      - '\regsvr32.exe'
      - '\rundll32.exe'
      - '\bitsadmin.exe'
      - '\wscript.exe'
      - '\cscript.exe'
      - '\msbuild.exe'
      - '\wmic.exe'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative use of these binaries or scripting engines
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Covers LOLBAS Binaries (certutil, mshta, regsvr32, rundll32, bitsadmin, wmic, msbuild) and Scripts (wscript, cscript). For library abuse (DLLs loaded via rundll32/regsvr32), inspect CommandLine for unusual .dll paths.

entries.title: A process has exited
entries.summary: Process termination event.
entries.details: Generated every time a process exits. Pair with 4688 using ProcessId to track full process lifetime. Useful for detecting short-lived processes indicative of payload execution or LOLBAS abuse (e.g., certutil.exe, mshta.exe, wscript.exe, or rundll32.exe completing after a single-shot payload).

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 4688 using ProcessId to calculate process lifetime — sub-second or very short-lived processes are characteristic of one-liner or stager execution
ExitStatus of non-zero may indicate the process failed or was killed — correlate with Defender or EDR telemetry
commonFalsePositives: Normal process termination for any application

entries.title: Backup of data protection master key was attempted
entries.summary: DPAPI master key backup attempted.
entries.details: Generated when a backup of the Data Protection API (DPAPI) master key is attempted. DPAPI master keys are used to encrypt secrets stored on disk — including browser saved passwords, Windows credential vault entries, private keys, and application secrets. A master key backup copies the key material to a domain controller (via MS-BKRP) or to a user-controlled location. Key fields: SubjectUserName and MasterKeyId. Unexpected master key backup attempts outside of normal domain join, user profile creation, or known backup software should be investigated. Attackers who obtain the DPAPI backup key from a domain controller can decrypt all domain user DPAPI-protected secrets offline.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Credentials from Password Stores: Windows Credential Manager

prerequisites.description: Audit DPAPI Activity must be enabled under Advanced Audit Policy > Object Access

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName identifies the account triggering the backup — unexpected accounts or non-interactive sessions are suspicious
MasterKeyId can be correlated with subsequent DPAPI decryption activity (EID 4695) to trace the full credential access chain
Correlate with Security EID 4624 (logon type) to determine whether the session was interactive or network-based
commonFalsePositives: Domain-joined machines automatically backing up master keys to the DC during normal user profile initialization
Enterprise backup software performing DPAPI key backup as part of authorized credential archival

entries.title: Recovery of data protection master key was attempted
entries.summary: DPAPI master key recovery attempted.
entries.details: Generated when recovery of a DPAPI master key is attempted. Recovery occurs when a master key is not available locally and must be retrieved from the domain controller backup (MS-BKRP) or from a user's roaming profile. Key fields: SubjectUserName and MasterKeyId. This event is directly linked to DPAPI-based credential theft tools such as Mimikatz's dpapi::masterkey /rpc command, which forces master key recovery from the domain controller. An attacker who can authenticate as the target user (or as a domain admin) can recover master keys for all users and then decrypt any DPAPI-protected secret. High-priority when fired by unexpected processes or outside of normal user logon activity.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Credentials from Password Stores: Windows Credential Manager

prerequisites.description: Audit DPAPI Activity must be enabled under Advanced Audit Policy > Object Access

investigationPivots: Correlate ProcessName with EID 4688 — Mimikatz, PowerShell, or unknown executables triggering master key recovery are high priority
SubjectUserName recovering a MasterKeyId associated with a different user account indicates impersonation or domain admin abuse
Correlate with EID 4624 logon type — recovery during a network logon (Type 3) is unusual and warrants investigation
commonFalsePositives: Users logging on to a new machine or after a master key rotation triggers automatic recovery from the domain backup
Profile synchronization in roaming profile environments

entries.title: Protection of auditable protected data was attempted
entries.summary: DPAPI encryption of auditable data was attempted.
entries.details: Generated when the CryptProtectData API is called to encrypt auditable protected data. This event tracks the encryption (wrapping) side of the DPAPI lifecycle. It is generated less frequently than EID 4695 and is most relevant when tracking applications storing new secrets under DPAPI — for example, a malicious implant storing its C2 configuration or exfiltrated credentials in DPAPI-encrypted blobs for later recovery. Key fields: SubjectUserName, ProcessName, and DataDescription. Correlate with EID 4695 to identify encryption-then-decryption patterns from non-standard applications.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Credentials from Password Stores: Windows Credential Manager

prerequisites.description: Audit DPAPI Activity must be enabled under Advanced Audit Policy > Object Access

investigationPivots: ProcessName is the primary pivot — applications outside of known browsers, credential managers, and system components encrypting auditable data warrant review
DataDescription field (when populated) identifies the type of secret being protected
Pair EID 4694 (encryption) with EID 4695 (decryption) from the same application to understand the full credential store lifecycle
commonFalsePositives: Browsers and email clients storing credentials or session tokens under DPAPI as part of normal operation
Windows systems encrypting user profile data, private keys, and stored passwords during normal activity

entries.title: Unprotection of auditable protected data was attempted
entries.summary: DPAPI decryption of auditable data was attempted.
entries.details: Generated when the CryptUnprotectData API is called to decrypt auditable DPAPI-protected data. This is the decryption (unwrapping) side of the DPAPI lifecycle and is the most forensically significant DPAPI event — it fires when a process reads DPAPI-encrypted secrets such as browser saved passwords, Windows Credential Manager vault entries, or application secrets. Key fields: SubjectUserName, ProcessName, and DataDescription. Mimikatz's dpapi::cred and dpapi::chrome commands trigger this event. An unexpected process or a process running under a different user context decrypting DPAPI blobs is a strong credential theft indicator.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Credentials from Password Stores: Credentials from Web Browsers
Credentials from Password Stores: Windows Credential Manager

prerequisites.description: Audit DPAPI Activity must be enabled under Advanced Audit Policy > Object Access

investigationPivots: ProcessName is the highest-value pivot — any non-browser, non-credential-manager process decrypting auditable DPAPI data warrants investigation
SubjectUserName decrypting data owned by a different user indicates impersonation or SYSTEM-level credential harvesting
Correlate with EID 4693 (master key recovery) and EID 5379 (Credential Manager read) to build a complete credential theft event chain
commonFalsePositives: Browsers decrypting stored passwords during normal autofill and form filling operations
Windows components decrypting user secrets during session initialization and service startup

entries.title: A service was installed in the system
entries.summary: Service installation recorded in Security log.
entries.details: Persistence indicator. Correlate with System 7045.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName identifies the account that installed the service — flag non-admin or unexpected accounts
Correlate with System EID 7045 for the ServiceFileName (executable path) to assess the binary location
ServiceType and ServiceStartType reveal whether the service runs as a kernel driver or user-mode process, and whether it starts at boot
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software installing services during setup
Windows Update adding or updating system services

detectionRules.title: Service Installed with Suspicious Binary Path
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4697
| where ServiceFileName !startswith "C:\\Windows\\"
| where ServiceFileName !startswith "C:\\Program Files\\"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, ServiceName, ServiceFileName, ServiceType, ServiceStartType
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Services installed outside Windows and Program Files directories are common indicators of malware persistence or lateral movement tools.

detectionRules.title: New Service Installed Outside Standard Paths
detectionRules.rule:
title: New Service Installed Outside Standard Paths
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4697
  filter:
    ServiceFileName|startswith:
      - 'C:\Windows\'
      - 'C:\Program Files\'
      - 'C:\Program Files (x86)\'
  condition: selection and not filter
falsepositives:
  - Third-party software installing services in non-standard locations
level: high

entries.title: A scheduled task was created
entries.summary: New scheduled task creation recorded.
entries.details: Generated every time a new scheduled task is created. High-fidelity persistence indicator. Examine TaskContent XML for command paths and LogonType. Tasks in the root Task Scheduler Library node are commonly associated with manual or malicious creation.

investigationPivots: TaskContent XML contains the full task definition — inspect the Action element for the executable path and arguments
LogonType of Password in the TaskContent stores credentials in Credential Manager; S4U or InteractiveToken are also worth noting
Tasks created in the root scheduler namespace (no subfolder) are more commonly malicious than vendor-subfoldered tasks
SubjectUserName identifies who created the task
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software installers creating scheduled tasks
Group Policy deploying tasks to managed endpoints

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Created in Root Namespace
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4698
| where TaskName !startswith "\\Microsoft\\"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, TaskName, TaskContent
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Tasks in \Microsoft\ subfolder are typically Microsoft/vendor-managed. Tasks at the root level (e.g., \TaskName) are more commonly attacker-created.

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Created
detectionRules.rule:
title: Scheduled Task Created
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4698
  filter:
    TaskName|startswith: '\Microsoft\'
  condition: selection and not filter
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate software installers creating scheduled tasks
  - Group Policy deploying tasks
level: medium

entries.title: A scheduled task was deleted
entries.summary: Scheduled task deletion recorded.
entries.details: Generated every time a scheduled task is deleted. Deletion of root-level tasks may indicate cleanup of malicious persistence. Correlate with 4698 to trace task lifecycle.

investigationPivots: Correlate TaskName with EID 4698 to determine when the task was created and whether it ran before deletion
Cross-reference with Task Scheduler EID 200 to confirm whether the task executed before being deleted — execution followed by deletion is a strong cleanup indicator
SubjectUserName identifies who deleted the task
commonFalsePositives: Software uninstallers removing their own scheduled tasks
One-time setup tasks configured to self-delete after completion

entries.title: A scheduled task was enabled
entries.summary: Scheduled task enabled.
entries.details: Generated every time a scheduled task is enabled. Monitor for re-enabling of previously disabled tasks, especially tasks located in the root Task Scheduler Library node.

investigationPivots: Re-enabling a previously disabled task may indicate repurposing of a dormant persistence mechanism
Correlate TaskName with EID 4698 to review the original task definition and action
SubjectUserName combined with timing — enablement outside business hours or during an active incident is suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy or software re-enabling tasks that were disabled during maintenance
IT re-enabling tasks after troubleshooting

entries.title: A scheduled task was updated
entries.summary: Scheduled task modified.
entries.details: Generated every time a scheduled task is updated. Examine TaskContentNew XML for changes to the command or LogonType. A LogonType of Password stores credentials in Credential Manager in cleartext.

investigationPivots: TaskContentNew XML shows the updated definition — diff the Action element against EID 4698 TaskContent to identify what changed
Changes to the executable path or arguments of an existing task are a high-fidelity persistence modification indicator
SubjectUserName and timing of the update relative to other suspicious activity helps establish attacker presence
commonFalsePositives: Software auto-updaters modifying their own scheduled task definitions
Group Policy refreshes updating task configurations

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Modified — Executable Path Changed
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4702
| where TaskName !startswith "\\Microsoft\\"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, TaskName, TaskContent
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Inspect TaskContent XML for changes to the Action/Command element. Correlate with EID 4698 for the original task definition to identify what changed.

entries.title: A user right was adjusted
entries.summary: A token privilege was enabled or disabled for a process.
entries.details: Generated when a token privilege (user right) is enabled or disabled during a process execution. Key fields: SubjectUserName, TargetUserName, TargetProcessName, EnabledPrivilegeList, and DisabledPrivilegeList. Attackers enabling high-value privileges such as SeDebugPrivilege (needed for LSASS dumping), SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege, or SeTcbPrivilege on unexpected processes is a credential access or privilege escalation indicator. This event is generated frequently by OS processes and requires filtering on high-risk privilege names.

investigationPivots: EnabledPrivilegeList is the key field — SeDebugPrivilege, SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege, SeLoadDriverPrivilege, and SeTcbPrivilege are the highest-risk privileges to monitor
Correlate TargetProcessName with Security EID 4688 to verify the process is legitimate
SeDebugPrivilege enabled on a non-system process accessing LSASS may precede credential dumping
commonFalsePositives: High volume from OS processes — filtering on specific high-risk privilege names is required to reduce noise
Debuggers and profiling tools legitimately enabling SeDebugPrivilege

entries.title: A user right was assigned
entries.summary: A privilege or user right was assigned to an account.
entries.details: Generated when a user right (privilege) is assigned to an account. Key fields: SubjectUserName (who made the change), TargetSid (the account receiving the right), and PrivilegeList (the specific privilege assigned). Assigning powerful privileges — such as SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeCreateTokenPrivilege, or SeImpersonatePrivilege — to accounts that should not have them is a privilege escalation technique. Changes to privilege assignments outside of normal change windows warrant investigation.

investigationPivots: PrivilegeList is the key field — SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeCreateTokenPrivilege, and SeImpersonatePrivilege on non-admin accounts are high risk
SubjectUserName identifies who assigned the right — changes made by unexpected accounts are suspicious
Correlate with EID 4672 for the account that subsequently uses the elevated privileges
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy applying standard privilege assignments during system startup or policy refresh
Software installers assigning service-specific privileges to service accounts

entries.title: A user right was removed
entries.summary: A privilege or user right was removed from an account.
entries.details: Generated when a user right is removed from an account. Key fields: SubjectUserName, TargetSid, and PrivilegeList. While adding privileges (EID 4704) is more commonly malicious, removing privileges from security-sensitive accounts or service accounts can be a defense evasion technique — for example, removing SeAuditPrivilege from an account to prevent it from generating audit events, or removing privileges from a security tool's service account to impair its function.

investigationPivots: TargetSid identifies whose privileges were removed — changes to security tool service accounts or admin accounts warrant investigation
SeAuditPrivilege removal from a service account may be an attempt to suppress event logging by that service
Correlate with EID 4704 to find privilege assignments/removals in close succession, which may indicate privilege manipulation
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy removing privileges from accounts during policy refresh
Software uninstallers removing privileges previously granted to service accounts

entries.title: A new trust was created to a domain
entries.summary: A new domain trust relationship was established.
entries.details: Generated when a new trust relationship is created between domains or forests. Key fields: SubjectUserName (who created the trust), TdoName (the trusted domain), TdoType (trust type: forest, external, realm, shortcut, cross-organizational), and TdoDirection (inbound, outbound, bidirectional). Domain trusts are a significant persistence and lateral movement vector — an attacker with Domain Admin rights can establish a rogue trust to an attacker-controlled domain to maintain persistent access even after credential rotation. Forest trusts grant extensive access across all domains in both forests. New trusts created outside of planned domain consolidation or partner federation activities should be treated as incidents.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Trust Modification

prerequisites.description: Audit Account Management > Audit Authentication Policy Change must be enabled. Must be collected from domain controllers.

investigationPivots: TdoName identifies the trusted domain — verify it is a known partner domain or internal forest
TdoType of 'Forest' or 'External' with an unexpected domain name is the highest fidelity persistence indicator
TdoDirection of 'Inbound' means the remote domain trusts this domain; 'Outbound' or 'Bidirectional' grants access to the remote domain
Correlate SubjectUserName with EID 4688 and EID 4624 to determine how the attacker obtained Domain Admin rights
commonFalsePositives: Planned trust creation during domain migrations, mergers, or partner federation projects
IT administrators setting up test or lab domain trusts

entries.title: A trust to a domain was removed
entries.summary: A domain trust relationship was removed.
entries.details: Generated when a trust relationship between domains is removed. Key fields: SubjectUserName and TdoName. While less common than trust creation as a malicious action, trust removal can be used as a destructive action (disabling legitimate partner access) or as a cleanup step after an attack. An attacker who established a rogue trust (EID 4706) may later remove it during cleanup to eliminate persistence artifacts. Unexpected removal of production trusts can also cause authentication failures for users in the trusted domain, effectively denying access.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification: Trust Modification

prerequisites.description: Audit Account Management > Audit Authentication Policy Change must be enabled. Must be collected from domain controllers.

investigationPivots: Check whether this follows a recent EID 4706 from the same TdoName — removal of a recently created trust indicates cleanup after rogue trust persistence
Verify that SubjectUserName had authorized change management approval for the trust removal
Removal of a legitimate production trust causes cross-domain authentication failures — correlate with EID 4625 or Kerberos errors from affected users
commonFalsePositives: Planned removal of obsolete trusts during domain consolidation or decommissioning
IT project cleanup after completed partner federation

entries.title: Kerberos policy was changed
entries.summary: Kerberos domain policy setting was modified.
entries.details: Generated when a Kerberos policy setting is changed in the domain. Key fields: SubjectUserName and the old/new policy values. Critical Kerberos policy settings include: MaxTicketAge (maximum Kerberos TGT lifetime, default 10 hours), MaxRenewAge (maximum TGT renewal period, default 7 days), MaxServiceAge (maximum service ticket lifetime, default 600 minutes), and MaxClockSkew (maximum clock skew tolerance, default 5 minutes). Attackers who forge Golden Tickets may modify MaxRenewAge and MaxTicketAge to extend the forged ticket's validity window, or may change MaxClockSkew to accommodate replay attacks. Any change to these settings outside of documented security policy changes warrants immediate review.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Golden Ticket

prerequisites.description: Audit Account Management > Audit Authentication Policy Change must be enabled. Must be collected from domain controllers.

investigationPivots: MaxRenewAge or MaxTicketAge changes are the highest priority — extending these values enables longer-lived Golden Tickets
MaxClockSkew reduction can indicate preparation for a replay attack (EID 4649)
SubjectUserName performing this change should be a domain admin account with documented change approval — flag any unexpected accounts
commonFalsePositives: Planned security policy changes as part of hardening initiatives (e.g., reducing MaxTicketAge)
Domain administrator testing Kerberos settings in a lab or staging environment that was inadvertently applied to production

entries.title: System audit policy was changed
entries.summary: The security audit policy was modified.
entries.details: Generated when the system-level audit policy is changed. Key fields: SubjectUserName and the specific audit category and subcategory changes (AuditPolicyChanges). Attackers frequently disable auditing before performing malicious actions — for example, disabling account logon auditing before credential attacks, or disabling object access auditing before registry or file modifications. A change that reduces audit coverage (Success/Failure → No Auditing) on any critical category is a defense evasion indicator. This event itself is only generated if 'Audit Policy Change' auditing is enabled.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable Windows Event Logging

investigationPivots: AuditPolicyChanges field shows which subcategory was changed and from/to what level — changes reducing coverage to 'No Auditing' are the most critical
SubjectUserName identifies the account that made the change — non-admin accounts or unexpected accounts warrant immediate investigation
Correlate with Security EID 1102 (log cleared) and EID 4688 — audit policy changes followed by a log clear is a strong evidence destruction pattern
commonFalsePositives: Group Policy audit policy enforcement during gpupdate cycles
Automated security hardening scripts applying CIS or STIG audit baselines

detectionRules.title: Audit Policy Disabled on Critical Category
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4719
| where AuditPolicyChanges has "No Auditing"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, AuditPolicyChanges
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Changes reducing coverage to 'No Auditing' are the highest priority. Correlate with EID 1102 (log cleared) in the same time window for an evidence destruction pattern.

detectionRules.title: Audit Policy Disabled
detectionRules.rule:
title: Audit Policy Disabled
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4719
    AuditPolicyChanges|contains: 'No Auditing'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Group Policy audit enforcement during gpupdate cycles
level: high

entries.title: A user account was created
entries.summary: New user account created.
entries.details: Generated every time a new user object is created on domain controllers, member servers, and workstations. Monitor for unexpected account creation, unusual UAC flags, SID history population, or accounts with pre-set delegation.

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName identifies who created the account — flag creation by non-IT accounts or automated processes
TargetUserName (the new account name) — generic, numeric, or system-like names (e.g., svc_, admin$) created unexpectedly are suspicious
Correlate with EID 4732 to see if the new account was immediately added to a privileged group
commonFalsePositives: IT provisioning new user accounts as part of onboarding
Automated provisioning systems creating service or application accounts

detectionRules.title: User Account Created by Non-IT Account
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4720
| where SubjectUserName !endswith "$"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, TargetUserName, TargetDomainName
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Any account creation by a non-IT account or outside a provisioning window is suspicious. Correlate with EID 4732 to check if the new account was immediately added to a privileged group.

detectionRules.title: New User Account Created
detectionRules.rule:
title: New User Account Created
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4720
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - IT provisioning during onboarding
  - Automated provisioning systems
level: medium

entries.title: A user account was enabled
entries.summary: User account enabled.
entries.details: Generated when a user or computer object is enabled. Monitor for re-enabling of dormant, disabled, or previously quarantined accounts.

investigationPivots: TargetUserName identifies the re-enabled account — check whether it is a dormant, terminated employee, or service account
SubjectUserName identifies who enabled the account
Re-enabling an account immediately followed by a logon event (EID 4624) from an unexpected source is a strong compromise indicator
commonFalsePositives: IT re-enabling accounts for users returning from leave
Automated processes re-enabling accounts after lockout resolution

entries.title: An attempt was made to change an account's password
entries.summary: User attempted to change their own password.
entries.details: Generated when an account attempts to change its own password (as opposed to an admin resetting another account's password, which generates EID 4724). Key fields: SubjectUserName (the user attempting the change) and TargetUserName (the account being changed, typically the same). The event fires regardless of success or failure; check the event outcome. Multiple failed password change attempts may indicate the user does not know the current password (possible account takeover where the attacker cannot change the password without knowing the original).

investigationPivots: Failed password change attempts on an account that the attacker controls may indicate they need a reset (EID 4724) to proceed
Unexpected password changes outside of normal IT processes or self-service portals warrant review
Correlate with EID 4624 to confirm whether the account was actively used before and after the change
commonFalsePositives: Users changing their own passwords at regular intervals per security policy
Password expiration prompts triggering self-service password changes

entries.title: An attempt was made to reset an account's password
entries.summary: Admin or privileged account reset another account's password.
entries.details: Generated when an account's password is reset by another account (administrative reset), as opposed to EID 4723 (user self-service change). Key fields: SubjectUserName (the account performing the reset) and TargetUserName (the account being reset). Password resets on privileged accounts — domain admin, service accounts, or accounts with elevated permissions — are a common account takeover indicator. Resets performed outside of IT helpdesk hours, or by accounts not typically involved in password management, warrant investigation.

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName is the key pivot — resets performed by unexpected accounts (non-IT, non-admin) are suspicious
TargetUserName identifies the victim account — resets targeting admin, service, or break-glass accounts are highest priority
Correlate with EID 4624 to determine if the target account was subsequently used to log on
commonFalsePositives: IT helpdesk resetting user passwords after user lockout or support requests
Automated provisioning systems resetting service account passwords

entries.title: A user account was disabled
entries.summary: User account disabled.
entries.details: Generated when a user or computer object is disabled. Useful for detecting disabling of service accounts or security tooling accounts as a precursor to attack.

investigationPivots: Disabling of a security service account (e.g., an EDR or logging service account) may indicate defense evasion
SubjectUserName identifies who disabled the account — flag unexpected actors
Correlate with EID 7036 (service stopped) if the disabled account is associated with a service
commonFalsePositives: IT disabling accounts as part of offboarding procedures
Automated systems disabling accounts due to policy (e.g., inactivity-based disabling)

entries.title: A user account was deleted
entries.summary: User account deleted.
entries.details: Generated every time a user object is deleted on domain controllers, member servers, and workstations. Monitor for deletion of service, administrative, or recently created accounts.

investigationPivots: Correlate TargetUserName with EID 4720 — deletion of a recently created account may indicate attacker cleanup
Deletion of a service or admin account that was never supposed to be removed warrants investigation
SubjectUserName identifies who deleted the account
commonFalsePositives: IT deprovisioning accounts during offboarding
Automated systems removing stale or expired accounts

entries.title: A member was added to a security-enabled global group
entries.summary: Member added to global security group.
entries.details: Generated when a member is added to a security-enabled global group such as Domain Admins. Pivot on TargetUserName (the group name) and MemberSid. High-value alert for additions to privileged groups.

investigationPivots: TargetUserName is the group name — additions to Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, or Schema Admins are critical priority
MemberSid identifies the added account — resolve the SID to determine whether it is a known account
SubjectUserName identifies who made the change — flag non-IT accounts or automated processes
commonFalsePositives: IT adding users to groups as part of role-based access provisioning

detectionRules.title: Member Added to Privileged Global Group
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4728
| where TargetUserName in ("Domain Admins", "Enterprise Admins", "Schema Admins", "Group Policy Creator Owners")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, TargetUserName, MemberSid
| order by TimeGenerated desc

detectionRules.title: Member Added to Privileged Domain Group
detectionRules.rule:
title: Member Added to Privileged Domain Group
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4728
    TargetUserName:
      - 'Domain Admins'
      - 'Enterprise Admins'
      - 'Schema Admins'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Authorized IT provisioning
level: high

entries.title: A member was removed from a security-enabled global group
entries.summary: Member removed from global security group.
entries.details: Generated when a member is removed from a security-enabled global group. Monitor for removal from privileged groups such as Domain Admins. Pivot on TargetUserName (group name) and MemberSid.

investigationPivots: Removal from Domain Admins or other privileged groups — correlate with EID 4728 to determine if this is part of an add/remove pattern (attacker covering tracks)
MemberSid identifies the removed account — check whether removal was expected or authorized
Removal of a security service account from a group may impair its function
commonFalsePositives: IT removing users from groups during role changes or offboarding

entries.title: A security-enabled local group was created
entries.summary: A local security group was created.
entries.details: Generated when a new security-enabled local group is created. Key fields: SubjectUserName and the new group name. Attackers create local groups to facilitate lateral movement or persistence — for example, adding a backdoor account to a newly created local group with specific permissions. Local group creation outside of standard IT provisioning processes warrants investigation, particularly if followed by member additions (EID 4732).

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 4732 to see which accounts were immediately added to the new group
SubjectUserName identifies who created the group — non-IT or non-admin accounts creating groups warrant investigation
New groups with names mimicking system groups (e.g., 'Administratores' with a typo) are a masquerading indicator
commonFalsePositives: IT provisioning scripts creating application-specific local groups
Software installers creating local groups for service accounts

entries.title: A member was added to a security-enabled local group
entries.summary: Member added to local security group.
entries.details: Generated when a new member is added to a security-enabled local group. Critical for monitoring additions to the local Administrators group. Pivot on TargetUserName (group name) and MemberSid.

investigationPivots: TargetUserName of Administrators is the highest priority — local admin access enables full control of the system
MemberSid of a domain account added to local Administrators is a common post-exploitation privilege escalation step
SubjectUserName identifies who added the member — flag unexpected actors
commonFalsePositives: IT adding service accounts or admin users to local groups as part of deployment
Software installers requiring local admin membership for their service accounts

detectionRules.title: Member Added to Local Administrators Group
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4732
| where TargetUserName == "Administrators"
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, MemberSid
| order by TimeGenerated desc

detectionRules.title: Member Added to Local Administrators Group
detectionRules.rule:
title: Member Added to Local Administrators Group
status: stable
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID: 4732
    TargetUserName: 'Administrators'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - IT adding service or admin accounts during deployment
level: high

entries.title: A member was removed from a security-enabled local group
entries.summary: Member removed from local security group.
entries.details: Generated when a member is removed from a security-enabled local group. Monitor for unexpected removals from critical groups such as local Administrators. Pivot on TargetUserName (group name) and MemberSid.

investigationPivots: Removal from Administrators group — may indicate cleanup after temporary privilege escalation
Correlate with EID 4732 to identify an add/remove pattern on the same account (attacker adding and then removing themselves)
MemberSid of a service or security account unexpectedly removed may impair system defenses
commonFalsePositives: IT removing users from local groups during offboarding or role changes

entries.title: A security-enabled local group was deleted
entries.summary: A local security group was deleted.
entries.details: Generated when a security-enabled local group is deleted. Key fields: SubjectUserName and the deleted group name. Group deletion as part of attack cleanup — removing a backdoor group created during the attack — is a defense evasion technique. Correlate EID 4734 with EID 4731 to identify groups that were created and then deleted within a short window, which is a strong indicator of malicious group manipulation.

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 4731 — group deleted shortly after creation is a strong cleanup indicator
Check EID 4732/4733 between the creation and deletion to identify what members were added and removed
SubjectUserName performing unexpected group deletions warrants investigation
commonFalsePositives: Software uninstallers removing application-specific local groups
IT cleanup scripts removing obsolete groups

entries.title: A security-enabled local group was changed
entries.summary: A local security group's attributes were modified.
entries.details: Generated when a security-enabled local group's attributes (such as description or SAM account name) are changed. Key fields: SubjectUserName and the changed group name. Note that member additions and removals generate EID 4732/4733 rather than this event — EID 4735 captures changes to the group object itself. Renaming a group to mimic a legitimate system group is a masquerading technique.

investigationPivots: Group name changes (SAM name) may indicate masquerading — compare the old and new names
SubjectUserName identifies who modified the group — unexpected accounts making group changes warrant investigation
Correlate with EID 4732 to see if member additions followed the group modification
commonFalsePositives: IT renaming groups as part of organizational restructuring
Software updates modifying their own local groups

entries.title: A user account was changed
entries.summary: User account attributes modified.
entries.details: Generated every time a user object is changed. A separate event is generated per attribute change. Unchanged attributes appear as '-'. Monitor UAC flag changes (e.g., Trusted For Delegation enabled), password changes on critical accounts, and AllowedToDelegateTo modifications.

investigationPivots: UserAccountControl flag changes are the highest priority — enabling Trusted For Delegation or DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH are delegation/AS-REP roasting setup indicators
AllowedToDelegateTo modifications set up constrained delegation and can be used for privilege escalation
TargetUserName combined with SubjectUserName — changes made by an account to a different account's properties are suspicious
commonFalsePositives: IT managing user account attributes as part of normal AD administration
Automated provisioning systems updating account properties

entries.title: A user account was locked out
entries.summary: User account locked out.
entries.details: Generated every time a user account is locked out. CallerComputerName identifies the machine that triggered the lockout. Common indicator of password spray, brute force, or misconfigured service credentials.

investigationPivots: CallerComputerName identifies the source machine — investigate that host for the authentication activity that caused lockouts
Multiple accounts locked out around the same time from the same source indicates a password spray
Correlate with EID 4625 on the CallerComputerName to identify the failing authentication attempts
commonFalsePositives: Misconfigured services using stale credentials triggering lockouts
Users forgetting their password after a recent change

entries.title: A computer account was created
entries.summary: A new computer account was created in Active Directory.
entries.details: Generated when a new computer (machine) account is created in Active Directory. Key fields: SubjectUserName (who created it) and the new computer account name. Attackers with appropriate permissions (e.g., default domain users can add up to 10 machine accounts) may create rogue computer accounts for Kerberos-based attacks such as Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD) abuse or silver ticket attacks. Computer accounts created by non-admin users, or with unusual naming conventions, warrant investigation.

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName creating a computer account is the key pivot — non-admin users creating machine accounts is a Kerberos abuse precursor
Computer account names with random strings or suspicious patterns (e.g., 'EVILPC$') are suspicious
Correlate with Kerberos EID 4768/4769 to see if the new computer account is immediately used for authentication
commonFalsePositives: IT joining new machines to the domain (typically performed via domain join, which creates a computer account)
MDM and provisioning tools creating computer accounts during enrollment

entries.title: A computer account was changed
entries.summary: A computer account's attributes were modified.
entries.details: Generated when a computer account's attributes are modified. Key fields: SubjectUserName, TargetComputerName, and the changed attributes. From a DFIR perspective, the most critical changes are modifications to msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity (enabling Resource-Based Constrained Delegation on the target account — a key step in RBCD abuse) and changes to the computer account's SPN list. These changes facilitate Kerberos delegation attacks that can lead to full machine compromise.

investigationPivots: Changes to msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute enable RBCD — this is a high-priority indicator when modified by unexpected accounts
SPN additions or removals may indicate Kerberoasting setup or Silver Ticket preparation
SubjectUserName making modifications to DC or high-value server computer accounts warrants immediate investigation
commonFalsePositives: Domain join operations and machine account updates during system startup
Legitimate Kerberos constrained delegation configuration by IT administrators

entries.title: A computer account was deleted
entries.summary: A computer account was removed from Active Directory.
entries.details: Generated when a computer account is deleted from Active Directory. Key fields: SubjectUserName and TargetComputerName. Deletion of recently created computer accounts (created for Kerberos abuse) as part of cleanup is a defense evasion technique. Correlate EID 4743 with EID 4741 to identify computer accounts created and deleted within a short timeframe.

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 4741 — computer account deleted shortly after creation indicates cleanup of a Kerberos abuse artifact
SubjectUserName performing unexpected computer account deletions, especially for active machines, warrants investigation
Check EID 4742 between creation and deletion to identify what attributes were modified
commonFalsePositives: IT removing stale machine accounts during cleanup operations
Decommissioned machines having their accounts removed from Active Directory

entries.title: A member was added to a security-enabled universal group
entries.summary: Member added to universal security group.
entries.details: Generated when a member is added to a security-enabled universal group. Universal groups span multiple domains, so additions can affect enterprise-wide permissions. Pivot on TargetUserName and MemberSid.

investigationPivots: Universal group membership affects all domains in the forest — additions to Enterprise Admins or universal privileged groups have the broadest impact
MemberSid identifies the added account — resolve the SID and check whether the account is known and authorized
SubjectUserName identifies who made the change
commonFalsePositives: IT provisioning users into forest-wide groups as part of role assignments

detectionRules.title: Member Added to Privileged Universal Group
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4756
| where TargetUserName in ("Enterprise Admins", "Schema Admins")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, SubjectUserName, SubjectDomainName, TargetUserName, MemberSid
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Universal groups span all domains in the forest. Enterprise Admins and Schema Admins in universal scope have the broadest impact.

entries.title: A member was removed from a security-enabled universal group
entries.summary: Member removed from universal security group.
entries.details: Generated when a member is removed from a security-enabled universal group. Universal groups span multiple domains, so removals may affect enterprise-wide permissions. Pivot on TargetUserName (group name) and MemberSid.

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 4756 to detect add/remove patterns — an account added and then removed may indicate temporary privilege use
Removal from Enterprise Admins or equivalent universal groups is unusual and warrants validation
MemberSid identifies the removed account
commonFalsePositives: IT removing users from forest-wide groups during role changes or offboarding

entries.title: SID History was added to an account
entries.summary: SID History attribute was added to a user or computer account.
entries.details: Generated when a SID (Security Identifier) is added to the SidHistory attribute of an Active Directory account. SID History is a legitimate feature used during domain migrations to preserve access rights for migrated users — it allows an account in a new domain to retain access to resources in the old domain by carrying the old SID. However, it is also a well-known persistence technique: an attacker with Domain Admin rights can add the SID of any privileged group (including Domain Admins or Enterprise Admins) to an arbitrary account's SID History, granting that account the same access rights as the target SID without modifying group membership. This technique is difficult to detect through group membership queries alone and survives credential resets. Key fields: SubjectUserName, TargetUserName, and SidList.

prerequisites.description: Audit Account Management > Audit User Account Management must be enabled. Must be collected from domain controllers.

investigationPivots: SidList reveals the SID(s) added — resolve the SID to identify whether it belongs to a privileged group such as Domain Admins (S-1-5-21-...-512)
TargetUserName is the account receiving the backdoor privilege — audit all access by this account since the SID History modification
SubjectUserName must have had Domain Admin rights to perform this operation — trace how this account was compromised
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate domain migration operations using tools such as ADMT (Active Directory Migration Tool)
Automated migration scripts with proper change management approval

entries.title: An attempt to add SID History to an account failed
entries.summary: Failed attempt to add SID History to an account.
entries.details: Generated when an attempt to add a SID to an account's SID History attribute fails. Key fields: SubjectUserName, TargetUserName, and the failure status. The failure may indicate insufficient privileges, auditing controls blocking SID History modification, or the operation being denied by a domain policy. While the attack was blocked, the event confirms an attempt was made — any unauthorized attempt to modify SID History should be treated as a high-severity incident indicating an attacker has or is attempting to gain domain-level persistence. Correlate with EID 4765 to determine whether other SID History modifications succeeded.

prerequisites.description: Audit Account Management > Audit User Account Management must be enabled. Must be collected from domain controllers.

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName attempted the operation — investigate how this account was compromised and what privilege level it held
Correlate with EID 4765 on all DCs — a failure on one DC may have succeeded on another
Check the failure reason — access denied failures confirm the account lacked privilege; other errors may indicate a partial attack
commonFalsePositives: Failed migration tool operations with misconfigured permissions during legitimate domain migrations

entries.title: A user account was unlocked
entries.summary: A locked-out user account was unlocked.
entries.details: Generated when a locked-out user account is unlocked by an administrator or automatically by the account lockout policy. Key fields: SubjectUserName (who unlocked it) and TargetUserName (the account that was unlocked). In attack contexts, this event is primarily useful for timeline reconstruction after a brute-force or password spray campaign: EID 4625 (repeated failures) → EID 4740 (account locked) → EID 4767 (admin unlocks) → EID 4624 (next successful logon). Automated unlock tools or scripts unlocking accounts in rapid succession across multiple accounts may indicate an attacker managing a spray campaign or attempting to bypass lockout policies.

prerequisites.description: Audit Account Management > Audit User Account Management must be enabled. Collected from domain controllers for domain accounts.

investigationPivots: SubjectUserName unlocking multiple accounts in rapid succession is suspicious — legitimate help desk unlocks are typically one at a time with a ticket
Correlate with EID 4740 to determine the caller computer name that triggered the lockout
A pattern of EID 4740 → EID 4767 → EID 4740 (repeat) may indicate an attacker repeatedly triggering lockouts and an admin repeatedly unlocking
commonFalsePositives: Help desk or IT administrators unlocking accounts as part of normal support operations
Automated account management tools with scheduled unlock windows

entries.title: The computer attempted to validate the credentials for an account
entries.summary: NTLM credential validation attempt recorded.
entries.details: Generated every time NTLM authentication is used, on the computer that holds the account. Captures all NTLM attempts on domain controllers for domain accounts. Monitor for repeated error codes (0xC000006A bad password, 0xC000006D generic failure) as brute-force indicators.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

investigationPivots: ErrorCode 0xC000006A (wrong password) in volume indicates brute force or pass-the-hash failure
Workstation field identifies the source machine — flag sources that are not domain-joined or are unexpected for the target account
NTLM authentication in an environment that should use Kerberos may indicate pass-the-hash or a non-domain-joined attacker host
commonFalsePositives: Legacy applications that do not support Kerberos and use NTLM
Workgroup machines and non-domain-joined systems authenticating via NTLM

entries.title: A session was reconnected to a Window Station
entries.summary: A session was reconnected to a Windows desktop session.
entries.details: Generated when a user reconnects to an existing Windows session (e.g., reconnecting to an RDP session or unlocking a workstation). Key fields: AccountName, ClientName (source hostname), ClientAddress (source IP), and SessionName. This event is useful for detecting RDP session hijacking — an attacker may reconnect to a legitimate user's disconnected RDP session (using tscon.exe or similar) without needing credentials. Unexpected reconnections from different source IPs than the original session, or reconnections to sessions belonging to other users, are strong lateral movement indicators.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Remote Service Session Hijacking: RDP Hijacking

investigationPivots: ClientAddress for the reconnection differing from the original connection's source IP is a session hijacking indicator
Reconnections to sessions owned by other users (AccountName different from expected) indicate RDP hijacking
Correlate with RDP Local Session Manager EID 25 (session reconnected) for additional session context
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate users reconnecting to their own RDP sessions from a different device
Users unlocking workstations after stepping away

entries.title: A session was disconnected from a Window Station
entries.summary: A session was disconnected from a Windows desktop session.
entries.details: Generated when a session is disconnected (but not logged off) from a window station. Key fields: AccountName, ClientName, ClientAddress, and SessionName. Disconnected sessions remain active in memory and are a target for session hijacking. Tracking disconnection events alongside EID 4778 (reconnection) provides a complete picture of session lifecycle. An attacker may disconnect a legitimate user's session before reconnecting to it.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Remote Service Session Hijacking: RDP Hijacking

investigationPivots: A user's session being disconnected and then immediately reconnected by a different source IP is a session hijack pattern
Disconnected sessions with no subsequent logoff (EID 4634/4647) remain active and can be hijacked
Correlate with RDP Local Session Manager EID 24 for additional disconnection context
commonFalsePositives: Normal RDP session disconnections when users close the RDP client without logging off
Network interruptions causing RDP sessions to disconnect

entries.title: The name of an account was changed
entries.summary: A user or computer account was renamed.
entries.details: Generated when an account is renamed. Key fields: SubjectUserName, OldTargetUserName (the original name), and NewTargetUserName (the new name). Renaming built-in accounts (e.g., renaming the Administrator account) is a common defense evasion technique to avoid detection rules that alert on 'Administrator' logons. Renaming accounts to mimic system or service accounts is a masquerading technique.

investigationPivots: OldTargetUserName to NewTargetUserName is the key pivot — renaming the default Administrator or Guest account is a classic evasion technique
